telltalePatterns: realization of /w/ as a labiodental fricative [v]
substitution of the dental fricative /θ/ with the alveolar stop [t]
substitution of the dental fricative /ð/ with the alveolar stop [d]

prosody.fluencyNotes: Fluent, with mostly natural phrase breaks. There is a tendency towards clearer articulation of individual words rather than extensive connected speech processes.

strengths: Consistent and clear articulation of most consonants and vowels, contributing to general comprehensibility.
Good overall fluency with a steady speech rate, preventing major processing demands on the listener.
Accurate placement of primary lexical stress in the majority of multi-syllabic words.

recommendedFocus.reason: The consistent substitution of /θ/ with [t] and /ð/ with [d] is a primary marker of L1 interference that significantly impacts intelligibility and can lead to misunderstandings (e.g., 'think' vs 'tink', 'weather' vs 'vedder').
The realization of /w/ as a labiodental fricative [v] affects clarity and can cause confusion, particularly with minimal pairs (e.g., 'wet' vs 'vet', 'was' vs 'vas').

The non-native realization of the English /ɹ/ as a vocalized [ɐ] or a trilled/tapped [r], especially in rhotic contexts, contributes to a noticeable accent, though it rarely impedes direct understanding.

result.transcript: Yesterday, the weather was really beautiful. So I decided to go for a walk through the park near my house. The trees were green, and the birds were singing everywhere. I think spring is my favorite time of the year. I walked along the river and watched some children playing with a big red ball. They were laughing and having so much fun together. One little girl threw the ball very high, and it landed right in the water. Her father had to reach in and pull it out. After that, I stopped at a small village shop to buy some bread and a bottle of juice.
